serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving is approximately 300 g, representing a shared plate of winkle snails coated in salted egg sauce, commonly ordered as a main or side dish at a Vietnamese seafood restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Whelk (boiled) and salted egg yolk sauce — composite estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central: Snails, raw (FDC ID 175177) and Egg, yolk, raw, salted (FDC ID 172185), with butter and evaporated milk components
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct HPB Singapore or FAO/INFOODS SEA entry exists for winkle snail with salted egg sauce. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data: snails (raw, FDC 175177), salted egg yolk (FDC 172185), butter (FDC 173410), and evaporated milk (FDC 171278). The dish is approximately 55% snail meat, 20% salted egg yolk sauce, 15% butter/cream base, and 10% aromatics (garlic, chilli, curry leaves). Values represent a weighted composite per 100 g of the finished dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — shellfish and protein-dominant seafood dishes
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for winkle snail with salted egg sauce. GI is estimated at 5 based on analogous high-protein, low-carbohydrate shellfish dishes (e.g., cooked mussels, oysters) from the University of Sydney GI Database, which consistently show GI values below 10 due to negligible glycemic carbohydrate content. Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (5 × 5.8 × 3) / 100 ≈ 0.87, rounded to 1.0. gi_category is low per University of Sydney classification (GI < 55 = low).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from salted egg yolk and sauce preparation
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its estimated sodium content of approximately 520 mg per 100 g (approximately 1,560 mg per standard 300 g serving), which represents over 65% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content and very low glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish has a low carbohydrate content of approximately 5.8 g per 100 g and an estimated glycemic index of 5, making it a low-glycemic-load option suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in moderate portions; however, the high sodium and saturated fat content from the salted egg sauce warrant attention to overall dietary balance.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated cholesterol, saturated fat, and sodium from salted egg yolk sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish sparingly, as the salted egg yolk sauce contributes approximately 185 mg cholesterol and 4.1 g saturated fat per 100 g, alongside high sodium levels, all of which are established risk factors for adverse cardiovascular outcomes.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Winkle snail with salted egg sauce (Vietnamese: Ốc mỡ sốt trứng muối) is a Vietnamese seafood dish featuring winkle snails — small, spiral-shelled marine gastropod molluscs — cooked and coated in a rich, creamy salted duck egg yolk sauce. The dish is popular in Vietnam as a street food and restaurant delicacy, and is also served in Vietnamese restaurants in Singapore. The sauce is made by combining mashed salted duck egg yolks with butter, evaporated milk, garlic, chilli, and curry leaves, creating a golden, umami-rich, slightly savoury and mildly spicy coating that clings to the snail shells. The texture of the snail meat is firm and slightly chewy, contrasting with the smooth, buttery sauce. Nutritionally, the dish is high in protein (approximately 12.5 g per 100 g) and provides significant iron (3.2 mg), vitamin B12 (2.8 mcg), and selenium (18 mcg) from the snail meat. The salted egg yolk sauce contributes substantial saturated fat, cholesterol, and sodium, making this a dish of concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is very low (estimated GI 5) due to the minimal carbohydrate content, making it suitable for blood glucose management in Type 2 Diabetes when portion size is controlled. This dish is not halal due to the use of non-halal snails in many preparations. It is a culturally significant dish in Vietnamese cuisine, often enjoyed as a shared appetiser or main course.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for winkle snail with salted egg sauce was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for snails, salted egg yolk, butter, and evaporated milk using a weighted composite approach. GI is estimated by analogy from shellfish data in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation methodology and absence of a direct approved-source match.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central component entries (snails raw FDC 175177, salted egg yolk FDC 172185, butter FDC 173410, evaporated milk FDC 171278) weighted by approximate dish composition (55% snail meat, 20% salted egg yolk sauce, 15% butter/cream base, 10% aromatics). Direct measurement of this specific dish preparation is not available in any approved primary regional database. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central entries for the component ingredients used in this composite estimate. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data and are approximate. 4. The food_group has been corrected from the input value of 'Oils, seasonings and condiments' to 'Fish and seafood', as winkle snail with salted egg sauce is a seafood dish. 5. The cuisine is classified as Vietnamese based on the dish's Vietnamese name and origin, served at a Vietnamese restaurant in Singapore. The record is tagged Singapore as the serving context. 6. Halal status is marked false as winkle snails (Littorina littorea) are generally not considered halal under most Islamic jurisprudence schools, though this may vary. 7. GI estimated at 5 based on analogous shellfish data from the University of Sydney GI Database; glycemic_index set to 5 (low) as this is a protein-dominant, low-carbohydrate seafood dish. 8. All -1 sentinel values in per_100g represent nutrients not individually reported in the USDA FoodData Central source entries used for this composite estimate.
